perturbation.original_text: Member shall not, during the term of the Franchise Agreement or thereafter, communicate, divulge or use, for any purpose other than the operation of the Franchised Business, any confidential information, knowledge, trade secrets or know-how which may be communicated to Member or which Member may learn by virtue of Member's relationship with Franchisee.
perturbation.changed_text: Member shall endeavor not to, during the term of the Franchise Agreement or thereafter, communicate, divulge or use, for any purpose other than the operation of the Franchised Business, any confidential information, knowledge, trade secrets or know-how which may be communicated to Member or which Member may learn by virtue of Member's relationship with Franchisee.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' introduces ambiguity. 'Shall not' is a direct prohibition, whereas 'shall endeavor not to' suggests an attempt to avoid disclosure, but does not guarantee it, potentially allowing for unintentional or loosely controlled disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the intent of the Trade Secrets Act, which aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. By changing the language to 'shall endeavor not to', the agreement becomes less enforceable. The Trade Secret Act requires protection from any misappropriation, not just intentional.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor not to' weakens the enforceability of the non-disclosure agreement, potentially conflicting with the Trade Secret Act's objective to protect trade secrets from misappropriation, regardless of intent. The Economic Espionage Act of 1996, as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, underscores the importance of protecting proprietary information, suggesting that any weakening of this protection could be viewed as a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (c) Member covenants and agrees that during the Post-Term Period (defined below), except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member shall not, either directly or indirectly, own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, within a three (3) mile radius of the premises of your Franchised Business or within a three (3) mile radius of any Franchised Business then-operating or under construction to operate under the System.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) Member generally agrees that during the Post-Term Period (defined below), except as otherwise approved in writing by Franchisor, Member should not, either directly or indirectly, own, manage, engage in, be employed by, advise, make loans to, consult for, or have any other significant interest in any Competitive Business that is, or intends to operate, near a Franchised Business.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'covenants and agrees that... shall not' to 'generally agrees that... should not' and replacing 'within a three (3) mile radius' with 'near' and 'any other interest' to 'any other significant interest' weakens the enforceability of the non-compete clause. The original wording is definitive, creating a clear obligation. The modified wording is vague and subjective, allowing the member to argue that their actions do not violate the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While there are exceptions, the ambiguous wording weakens the enforceability of the non-compete clause to such an extent it would make it very difficult for an organization to have the clause enforced in the state of California. Specifically, replacing the defined 'three (3) mile radius' with the subjective 'near' makes it difficult to determine a geographical boundary, thereby contradicting the clarity required for an enforceable exception to the general prohibition of non-compete agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the non-compete clause, specifically by changing 'covenants and agrees that... shall not' to 'generally agrees that... should not' and replacing 'within a three (3) mile radius' with 'near'. California Business and Professions Code 16600(a) states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void'. The original contract attempted to define and limit this restraint with specific language, but the modified version weakens this limitation to the point where it becomes difficult to enforce and potentially violates the statute by not providing clear, enforceable restrictions, thus creating a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Member agrees to pay all court costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred by Franchisor in obtaining specific performance of, or an injunction against violation of, the requirements of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Member may be responsible for a portion of court costs and attorney's fees incurred by Franchisor if deemed appropriate by the court in actions related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the Member agrees to pay all court costs and attorney's fees, which is very definitive. The modification changes this to 'Member may be responsible for a portion of court costs and attorney's fees if deemed appropriate by the court.' This is far more ambiguous and allows for significant legal challenges regarding who pays for what.
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by State; Example: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1032
perturbation.law_explanation: Many states have 'prevailing party' statutes regarding attorney's fees. These laws often dictate that the winning party in a legal dispute is entitled to recover their attorney's fees and court costs from the losing party. By introducing the ambiguity of 'may be responsible for a portion' and 'if deemed appropriate by the court,' the modified text conflicts with the typical application of these prevailing party statutes, making it unclear whether the member is liable even if the franchisor prevails. This weakens the franchisor's legal recourse and deviates from established legal precedent regarding cost allocation in contract disputes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1032 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1032. (a) As used in this section, unless the context clearly requires otherwise: (1) “Complaint” includes a cross-complaint. (2) “Defendant” includes a cross-defendant, a person against whom a complaint is filed, or a party who files an answer in intervention. (3) “Plaintiff” includes a cross-complainant or a party who files a complaint in intervention. (4) “Prevailing party” includes the party with a net monetary recovery, a defendant in whose favor a dismissal is entered, a defendant where neither plaintiff nor defendant obtains any relief, and a defendant as against those plaintiffs who do not recover any relief against that defendant. If any party recovers other than monetary relief and in situations other than as specified, the “prevailing party” shall be as determined by the court, and under those circumstances, the court, in its discretion, may allow costs or not and, if allowed, may apportion costs between the parties on the same or adverse sides pursuant to rules adopted under Section 1034. (b) Except as otherwise express
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding the member's responsibility for court costs and attorney's fees, potentially conflicting with prevailing party statutes. The original text clearly states the Member agrees to pay 'all' costs, while the changed text makes it contingent ('may be responsible for a portion' and 'if deemed appropriate by the court'). This creates a contradiction because prevailing party statutes, as suggested in the Law Explanation, generally assign responsibility for these costs to the losing party. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CCP 1032) discusses the determination of the “prevailing party” and the court's discretion in awarding costs, however it also states that the prevailing party includes 'the party with a net monetary recovery'. The modified text introduces uncertainty in the allocation of costs, deviating from the potential standard application of awarding costs to the prevailing party, thus creating a contradiction. The Perturbation explanation confirms that the changed text now 'allows for significant legal challenges' regarding cost allocation.
